chunk_summary: Guidelines for feeding during the first 1000 days focus on exclusive breastfeeding (EBF) for 0–6 months, complementary feeding from 6 months onward, and responsive feeding practices to prevent stunting, anemia, and neurodevelopmental loss.
learning_objectives: Understand the importance of the first 1000 days in preventing stunting, anemia, and neurodevelopmental loss.
Learn age-specific feeding practices, including EBF for 0–6 months and complementary feeding from 6 months onward.
Identify practical counseling strategies for caregivers, including responsive feeding and addressing common feeding derailers.
Recognize the role of national programs like MAA, ICDS, and the National Iron Plus Initiative in supporting infant and young child feeding.
key_facts: The first 1000 days (conception to 2 years) are critical for preventing stunting, anemia, and neurodevelopmental loss.
Exclusive breastfeeding (EBF) is recommended for 0–6 months, with early initiation within 1 hour of birth and on-demand feeding.
Complementary feeding begins at 6 months to meet energy, iron, and zinc needs, while breastfeeding continues through 24 months or longer.
Responsive feeding involves recognizing hunger cues, feeding slowly, encouraging but not forcing, and stopping at satiety cues.
Common feeding derailers in India include discarding colostrum, prelacteals like honey or ghutti, early water introduction, and prolonged bottle use.
reference_quote: Exclusive breastfeeding (EBF) for 0–6 months, with early initiation within 1 hour and on-demand feeding, establishes supply and reduces neonatal morbidity. (WHO)
document_key_topics: India-specific feeding challenges and support programs
